policy_statement: Continuously develop procurement criteria and methods in construction and maintenance to reflect ambitious goals on energy efficiency, lifecycle CO2 emissions and environmental impacts.
mechanism_description: The City will develop procurement criteria for construction and maintenance that account for energy efficiency and lifecycle CO2, leading to lower lifecycle emissions from purchased construction works and services.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement set → ↓ high-carbon materials/processes + ↑ energy-efficient design → ↓ embodied + operational GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes how it buys/commissions construction and maintenance (tender specifications, award criteria, and contractor set) to prioritize low lifecycle CO₂ and high energy efficiency.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Suppliers and project teams substitute lower-carbon materials (e.g., lower-clinker concrete, recycled/biobased materials), reduce material quantities, improve construction methods/logistics, and deliver more energy-efficient assets, lowering fossil energy use and process emissions across the value chain.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced embodied emissions (materials manufacturing + construction) and reduced operational emissions (energy use during the asset life), yielding net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions from construction projects by requiring low-carbon/energy-efficient options through public procurement.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement criteria directly drive lower lifecycle GHG emissions from construction by changing material/process choices and building energy performance.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve resource efficiency (less material/waste) and sometimes resilience (better building performance), the mechanism’s explicit and largest climate effect is emissions reduction (embodied + operational).  
3) Edge cases applied: If procurement focused solely on circularity without clear GHG intent, Resource Efficiency could dominate; if focused solely on climate resilience standards, Adaptation could dominate. Here, lifecycle CO₂ and energy efficiency indicate mitigation-dominant intent and impact.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that uses green construction procurement criteria aimed at lifecycle CO₂/energy efficiency primarily functions by reducing construction-related GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any secondary categorization (e.g., circular economy co-benefits, air quality, resilience) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “lifecycle CO₂” is used only as a reporting requirement without influencing awards/requirements, actual mitigation impact may be weaker; if criteria prioritize adaptation features (e.g., flood-proofing) over emissions, the dominant pathway could shift.
key_indicators: "develop its criteria and methods for procurements"; "construction and maintenance"; "energy efficiency"; "lifecycle CO2 emissions"; "environmental impacts"; "City's ambitious goals"
co_benefits: Reduced broader environmental impacts (e.g., air pollution, resource use, waste), potential cost savings over asset lifecycles.
instance_edge_case_notes: "Constantly develop criteria and methods" signals an ongoing procurement strategy; while it doesn’t specify binding thresholds, it still functions as a procurement requirement because it explicitly updates tender criteria to reflect energy/lifecycle CO₂ goals.
